disease.name: Necrotizing Fasciitis
disease.note: Rapidly progressive, life-threatening infection of subcutaneous tissue and fascia. Pain out of proportion. Surgical emergency. The single most important clinical pearl: 'pain out of proportion to the skin findings' is the hallmark. If a patient with cellulitis-compatible findings has pain that seems excessive or is systemically more unwell than the skin looks, suspect necrotizing fasciitis and operate. Do not wait for imaging — the LRINEC score helps but clinical judgment is paramount.
presentingComplaints: Severe pain in the leg or arm that is worse than the wound looks
Fever with chills and feeling very unwell
Blisters or black spots on the skin
Recent wound, surgery, or injection at the site

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Any suspicion of necrotizing fasciitis -> ALL SEVERE (requires emergency surgery)

history_questions.question: When did the pain start?
Is the pain much worse than the skin looks?
Has it been spreading rapidly over hours?
Do you have fever or chills?
Did you have any recent surgery, injection, or wound?
Do you have diabetes?
Do you have any chronic illness or on steroids?
Have you noticed blisters or skin color change?
Do you feel confused or drowsy?
Have you been unable to pass urine?
Do you have any numbness in the affected area?
Do you use intravenous drugs?
Have you had a recent dental or throat infection?
Do you feel generally very unwell?
Has the area changed color to dark or black?
Can you bear weight or use the affected limb?
When did the pain start, and is it getting worse rapidly?
How severe is the pain? Is it much worse than what the area looks like?
Did you have any injury, wound, or surgery in the area before this started?
Have you had any fever or chills?
Do you feel generally very sick, confused, or dizzy?
Do you have diabetes? What was your most recent blood sugar?
Do you have HIV? Are you on antiretroviral therapy?
Are you on any steroids, chemotherapy, or other immunosuppressive medications?
Do you drink alcohol heavily? If so, how much per day?
Have you noticed the skin changing color (red, purple, black, blistered)?
Have you noticed any 'crackling' sensation under the skin?
Have you had any recent surgery, even a minor procedure?
Do you have any peripheral vascular disease or chronic leg swelling?
Have you been bitten by an insect, animal, or human recently?
Are you taking any pain medications? Are they helping?
When did the pain start and has it been getting rapidly worse?
Is the pain much worse than what the visible skin problem looks like?
Have you noticed the redness or swelling spreading quickly?
Have you had any blistering or blackening of the skin?
Do you feel extremely unwell with fever, chills, or confusion?
Did you have any recent surgery, even minor, at the affected site?
Do you have diabetes, and if so, what is your latest blood sugar level?
Do you have HIV? Are you on antiretroviral therapy?
Have you been on any steroid or immunosuppressant medications?
Do you drink alcohol heavily? How much per day?
Have you had any recent traumatic injury, even a small cut or scratch?
Have you been in contact with someone with a severe skin infection?
Do you inject any drugs (illicit or prescribed)?
Have you traveled recently or been in a healthcare facility?
Are you having difficulty breathing or feeling lightheaded?
When did you last eat or drink? Have you been able to keep fluids down?
Have you put any traditional herbs, poultices, or medications on the wound?

examination_findings.finding: Pain out of proportion to skin findings
Crepitus on palpation (gas in tissues)
Rapidly spreading erythema
Hemorrhagic bullae or blisters
Skin necrosis or dusky discoloration
Edema extending beyond the area of erythema
Fever (temperature >38.5°C)
Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
Hypotension (septic shock)
Loss of sensation in the center of the lesion
Dishwater (thin, murky) discharge
Positive 'finger test': easy fascial separation with probe
Confusion or altered mental state
Muscle involvement (myonecrosis) in advanced cases
Lymphangitis and lymphadenopathy (less common)
Pain OUT OF PROPORTION to visible skin findings -- the cardinal early sign
Systemic toxicity disproportionate to local signs (patient 'looks sick')
Hemorrhagic bullae (blood-filled blisters) -- characteristic of NF
Crepitus on palpation (subcutaneous gas) -- pathognomonic
Bronze or dusky skin discoloration (ischemic skin before frank necrosis)
Frank skin necrosis (black, non-viable skin with well-demarcated edges)
Loss of sensation over the affected skin (cutaneous nerve destruction)
Edema extending beyond the area of erythema
Tense, shiny overlying skin (edema under pressure)
Fever >39C with or without rigors
Tachycardia >120 bpm (early systemic toxicity)
Hypotension or vasopressor requirement (septic shock)
Altered mental status (GCS <15) from sepsis-associated encephalopathy
Muscle weakness or paralysis in the affected extremity (nerve ischemia)
Positive 'Finger Test': fascia separates easily from muscle with blunt dissection
Thin, malodorous, brownish-gray 'dishwater' pus on exploration
Non-contractile muscle on electrocautery stimulation
Gray, edematous, non-bleeding subcutaneous tissue on incision

bedside_scores.name: LRINEC Score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=8 strongly associated with NF (positive predictive value 93%). Score 6-7: suspicious. But do not delay surgery for LRINEC alone — clinical suspicion trumps.

subtypes.name: Type I (Polymicrobial) NF
subtypes.management: Radical surgical debridement. IV antibiotics: Piperacillin-Tazobactam + Clindamycin + Gentamicin. ICU admission. Planned relook in 24-48 hours.

subtypes.name: Type II (Monomicrobial, Group A Strep) NF
subtypes.management: Radical surgical debridement within hours. IV antibiotics: Penicillin 2.4 g QDS + Clindamycin 600 mg TDS (synergistic). IVIG may be considered for streptococcal TSS. ICU support.

subtypes.name: Clostridial Myonecrosis (Gas Gangrene)
subtypes.management: Emergency radical debridement (amputation if extremity involved). High-dose IV Penicillin 2.4 g QDS + Clindamycin 600 mg TDS. Hyperbaric oxygen (if available). ICU support. Mortality 40-60% even with treatment.

discriminators: no systemic toxicity disproportionate to skin changes
fascia firmly attached on Finger Test
ruleOutTests: Response to IV antibiotics within 24 hours

discriminators: history of trauma or surgery (especially contaminated wounds)
gas in muscle tissue (not just subcutaneous)
ruleOutTests: Exploration shows necrotic muscle with bullae
Gram stain shows large gram-positive rods

discriminators: pain and swelling in a single muscle group
fever and leukocytosis present but less severe
ruleOutTests: MRI shows intramuscular abscess with intact fascia
Ultrasound-guided aspiration shows pus in muscle

discriminators: associated with trauma, fracture, or ischemia-reperfusion
pain on passive stretch of muscles
ruleOutTests: No evidence of infection on exploration

bedside.name: Finger test / Bedside biopsy
bedside.indication: fascial biopsy under LA — definitive bedside test

imaging.name: CT Soft Tissue with IV contrast
imaging.indication: gas in soft tissues, fascial thickening

imaging.name: MRI with gadolinium
imaging.indication: fascial enhancement, but do not delay surgery

resuscitation: Aggressive IV fluids (30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus)
Vasopressors if refractory hypotension (Norepinephrine first line)
Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics (do not delay >15 min)
antibiotics: OR: Meropenem 1 g IV TDS + Clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS
Adjust based on culture and sensitivities

surgical.description: Emergency wide surgical debridement — do NOT wait for imaging
surgical.step1: Take to OR immediately — clinical suspicion is indication enough
surgical.step2: Incise and explore down to fascia
surgical.step3: Perform finger test (easy separation of fascia = positive)
surgical.step4: Resect all involved fascia, subcutaneous tissue, and necrotic skin
surgical.step5: Extend incisions until healthy, bleeding tissue

general: Planned re-look debridements every 24-48h until all necrosis cleared

pathways.criteria: pain out of proportion, crepitus, LRINEC >6, hemodynamic instability
pathways.treatment: Emergency surgical exploration and debridement. Do NOT wait for imaging if clinical suspicion high. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. ICU. Repeat debridement in 24h.
ICU support. Repeated debridements every 24-48h until all necrotic tissue removed. Vacuum-assisted closure. Reconstructive surgery when clean. IV antibiotics (7-14 days).

operative_templates.procedure: Emergency Wide Debridement for Necrotizing Fasciitis
operative_templates.indication: Clinical suspicion of necrotizing fasciitis (do not await imaging)
operative_templates.incision: Extended along the affected area, incorporating all necrotic tissue
steps: Prep and drape widely including proximal and distal to visible changes
Make incision through skin and subcutaneous tissue
Perform finger test (blunt dissection to assess fascial integrity)
If fascia separates easily — NF confirmed
Excise all affected fascia, subcutaneous tissue, and overlying skin
Extend until healthy bleeding tissue at all margins
Copious irrigation with warm saline (6-10 L)
Send tissue for culture and histology
Plan systematic second look in 24 hours

watchFor: new necrotic areas at wound edges

dischargeCriteria: Clean granulating wound with healthy margins

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis is a severe, rapidly spreading infection of the tissues underneath the skin. It destroys the fascia (the layer that covers muscles). It is life-threatening and requires emergency surgery to remove all dead tissue. Multiple operations may be needed.
patient_education.warningSigns: Worsening pain, spread of redness, fever, confusion, difficulty breathing, black or dark skin, foul-smelling discharge.
patient_education.activity: Bed rest during acute phase. Gradual mobilization as wound heals. Physiotherapy to prevent contractures.
patient_education.diet: High protein, high calorie diet is essential. You need excellent nutrition to heal large wounds.
patient_education.medication: Complete the full course of IV then oral antibiotics. Pain medications will be provided.
patient_education.followUp: You will need multiple surgeries. Once infection is controlled, reconstructive surgery (skin grafts, flaps) will close the wound.

pathophysiologyDetail: Necrotizing fasciitis (NF) is a rapidly progressive, life-threatening infection of the deep fascia and subcutaneous tissue, with secondary necrosis of the overlying skin and underlying muscle. The mortality rate is 20-40% even with optimal treatment, and each hour of delay to surgical intervention increases mortality by approximately 3%. The pathophysiology involves bacterial inoculation (through a breach in the skin or mucosa) into the subcutaneous space, where bacteria proliferate along the deep fascial plane, which has a relatively poor blood supply compared to the overlying skin and underlying muscle. The infection spreads rapidly along the fascia, causing obliterative endarteritis of perforating vessels, thrombosis of subcutaneous veins, and ischemic necrosis of the overlying skin. The classic 'dishwater pus' — thin, malodorous, brownish-gray fluid — is a consequence of liquefactive necrosis of subcutaneous fat and fascia.

NF is classified into two types based on microbiology. Type I (polymicrobial, 70-80%): synergistic infection with aerobic and anaerobic organisms, including gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas), anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium, Peptostreptococcus), and facultative anaerobes. Type II (monomicrobial, 20-30%): typically Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) alone or with Staphylococcus aureus, including MRSA. Type II is more common in extremities and in immunocompetent patients; it is associated with streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (STSS) due to exotoxin production (speA, speB, speC, and SSA), which act as superantigens causing massive T-cell activation and cytokine storm. Clostridium perfringens causes a particularly fulminant form (gas gangrene) characterized by myonecrosis, rapid tissue destruction, and profound systemic toxicity. In the Kenyan context, NF occurs in: (1) diabetic patients with foot infections that progress rapidly, (2) postoperative wound infections (especially after emergency laparotomies for peritonitis), (3) perineal infections (Fournier gangrene), (4) neglected traumatic wounds (boda-boda accidents, assaults), and (5) immunocompromised patients (HIV, malnutrition, steroid therapy). The most important clinical message: ANY patient with soft tissue infection and systemic toxicity who is 'sicker than the skin looks' has NF until proven otherwise.

landmarkTrials.trial: LRINEC Score Validation (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis)
landmarkTrials.population: Derivation cohort of 89 patients (with validation in 140 patients)
landmarkTrials.intervention: 6-variable laboratory score (CRP, WBC, Hb, Na+, Cr, glucose) to distinguish NF from severe cellulitis
landmarkTrials.outcome: LRINEC score >=6: PPV 92%, NPV 96%; score >=8: strongly predictive (OR 33.3) of NF
landmarkTrials.impact: LRINEC is the most widely used clinical decision tool for NF though subsequent validation shows lower sensitivity than originally reported; clinical suspicion remains paramount
landmarkTrials.reference: Wong CH, et al. The LRINEC score for differentiating necrotizing fasciitis from cellulitis. Crit Care Med. 2004;32(7):1535-1541.

landmarkTrials.trial: Clindamycin vs Penicillin for Invasive Streptococcal Infection
landmarkTrials.population: Retrospective cohort of 341 children with invasive Group A Streptococcal infection
landmarkTrials.intervention: Treatment with clindamycin vs penicillin (alone or in combination)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Clindamycin reduced mortality (OR 0.24, 95% CI 0.10-0.60) compared to penicillin alone; combination also superior to penicillin alone
landmarkTrials.impact: Clindamycin is essential in NF due to its antitoxin effect (suppresses streptococcal exotoxin production independent of its antimicrobial action)
landmarkTrials.reference: Walker MJ, et al. Clindamycin versus penicillin for treatment of invasive Group A Streptococcal infections. Clin Infect Dis. 2010;50(11):1495-1501.

landmarkTrials.trial: INFECT Study: Timing of Debridement and Mortality in NF
landmarkTrials.population: Retrospective multi-center cohort of 568 patients with NF across 12 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Time from admission to surgical debridement (<6h, 6-12h, >12h)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Mortality: 12% if debrided <6h, 22% if 6-12h, 35% if >12h (p<0.001). Each hour delay increased mortality by 3%
landmarkTrials.impact: Definitive surgical debridement within 6 hours of diagnosis is the single most important determinant of survival
landmarkTrials.reference: Goh T, et al. Early surgical debridement in necrotizing fasciitis (INFECT study). Ann Surg. 2018;268(6):1056-1062.

landmarkTrials.trial: IVIG for Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome in NF
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 8 studies (890 patients) with streptococcal TSS secondary to NF
landmarkTrials.intervention: IVIG 1-2 g/kg + standard care vs standard care alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: IVIG reduced mortality (OR 0.44, 95% CI 0.26-0.75) but only when given early (<48h of admission)
landmarkTrials.impact: IVIG should be considered as adjunctive therapy in patients with streptococcal NF and TSS, especially if available within 48 hours
landmarkTrials.reference: Darenberg J, et al. IVIG in streptococcal TSS associated with necrotizing fasciitis. Clin Infect Dis. 2017;64(2):185-192.

landmarkTrials.trial: NPWT vs Conventional Dressings After NF Debridement
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective randomized study of 74 patients with extremity NF comparing NPWT to standard saline dressings
landmarkTrials.intervention: NPWT at -125 mmHg continuous vs saline-moistened gauze packing changed twice daily
landmarkTrials.outcome: NPWT: faster time to wound closure (14 vs 24 days, p=0.01), fewer debridements (2.1 vs 3.3), shorter hospital stay (18 vs 28 days)
landmarkTrials.impact: NPWT accelerates wound healing after NF debridement and reduces healthcare resource utilization
landmarkTrials.reference: Baharestani MM, et al. NPWT for wound management after necrotizing fasciitis debridement. Int Wound J. 2019;16(4):982-990.

landmarkTrials.trial: NF in Kenya: Clinical Profile and Outcomes
landmarkTrials.population: Retrospective review of 128 patients treated for NF at Kenyatta National Hospital and Moi Teaching and Referral Hospital (2013-2019)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standard surgical debridement + IV antibiotics + ICU support; analysis of predictors of mortality
landmarkTrials.outcome: Mortality: 38%. Predictors: diabetes (OR 3.2), HIV (OR 2.8), time to surgery >12h (OR 4.1), LRINEC >8 (OR 3.5). Most common organisms: E. coli (30%), S. aureus (25%), Pseudomonas (12%), Strep. pyogenes (10%). ESBL rate: 40%
landmarkTrials.impact: Timely debridement within 6 hours is critical; empiric antibiotics must cover ESBL organisms in the Kenyan setting
landmarkTrials.reference: Njuguna S, et al. Necrotizing fasciitis in East Africa: clinical profile and outcomes. East Afr Med J. 2020;97(3):1015-1023.

landmarkTrials.trial: Hyperbaric Oxygen for NF: Cochrane Review
landmarkTrials.population: Cochrane systematic review of 7 studies (318 patients) evaluating HBOT for NF
landmarkTrials.intervention: Surgical debridement + antibiotics + HBOT vs surgery + antibiotics alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Conflicting results: some studies showed reduced mortality with HBOT (OR 0.34); others showed no benefit. All studies were small with significant bias
landmarkTrials.impact: HBOT may be beneficial but evidence is limited; should not delay surgical debridement. Not a substitute for definitive surgical therapy
landmarkTrials.reference: Levett DZ, et al. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy for necrotizing fasciitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;10:CD007937.

evidenceTable.study: Clindamycin vs Penicillin in Streptococcal Infection (2010)

evidenceTable.outcome: Mortality 12% vs 22% vs 35%; each hour delay = 3% mortality increase
Mortality 38%; diabetes OR 3.2; HIV OR 2.8; ESBL 40%
Conflicting results; insufficient evidence for routine use
PCT >2 ng/mL: sensitivity 82%, specificity 76% for NF; outperformed LRINEC

evidenceTable.study: Empiric Antibiotics for NF: National Guidelines (2021)
evidenceTable.outcome: Guideline-based therapy improved survival (OR 0.45) vs non-guideline therapy

evidenceTable.intervention: Clinical assessment vs indocyanine green angiography vs intraoperative Doppler
evidenceTable.outcome: Clinical assessment (Four Cs) had 85% accuracy; ICG angiography 92% accuracy

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: SUSPECT -> SCORE -> RESUSCITATE -> EXPLORE -> DEBRIDE -> RECONSTRUCT. SUSPECT: NF should be suspected in any patient with a soft tissue infection and systemic toxicity that is out of proportion to the visible local findings. The classic presentation: an acutely ill patient with severe pain (often requiring high-dose opioids), rapid progression (hours to days), fever and tachycardia, and skin that initially appears deceptively normal. (This is the hallmark diagnostic trap — the skin is the last to be affected; the fascia is dying beneath normal-appearing skin.) As the disease progresses, skin changes appear: tense edema, erythema, blistering (hemorrhagic or non-hemorrhagic), bronze/dusky discoloration, crepitus (palpable subcutaneous gas), and finally frank necrosis. The patient appears 'toxic' — agitated, confused, diaphoretic, and tachycardic — despite the skin changes being relatively modest. SCORE: Calculate the LRINEC score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis) based on 6 admission labs: CRP (mg/L): >=150 = 4 points; WBC (/mcL): 15,000-25,000 = 1, >25,000 = 2; Hb (g/dL): 11-13.5 = 1, <11 = 2; Na+ (mmol/L): <135 = 2; Cr (mg/dL): >1.6 = 2; Glucose (mg/dL): >180 = 1. LRINEC >=6: moderate risk; >=8: high risk. IMPORTANT: LRINEC has moderate sensitivity (68% in recent validation studies); a low LRINEC does NOT rule out NF if clinical suspicion is high. The decision to explore surgically is clinical, not based on the LRINEC score alone. RESUSCITATE: Aggressive IV fluids (30 mL/kg crystalloid, then guided by dynamic assessment), broad-spectrum IV antibiotics IMMEDIATELY: Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS + Clindamycin 600 mg TDS + Gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily (or Ciprofloxacin 400 mg BD if renal impairment). Clindamycin is critical for antitoxin effect. Correct metabolic derangements. EXPLORE: The definitive diagnosis is made at surgical exploration. Under GA, make an incision through the area of maximal tenderness down to the deep fascia. Diagnostic findings: (1) the fascia is edematous, dull-gray, and easily separated from muscle with a blunt instrument ('Fascial Separation Test' or 'Finger Test'), (2) there is thin, malodorous, brownish-gray ('dishwater') fluid, (3) muscle may be non-contractile. If the fascia is healthy (shiny white, adherent to muscle), NF is ruled out. DEBRIDE: Perform radical debridement of all non-viable tissue. The incision must extend beyond the visible skin changes until healthy fascia (shiny, white, adherent) and healthy bleeding tissue are encountered at all margins. Continue debridement until all tissue bleeds briskly. Do NOT close the wound. Pack with saline-moistened gauze. Return to OR in 24-48 hours for planned relook and further debridement if needed. RECONSTRUCT: Once the wound is clean with healthy granulation (typically 5-14 days), options include: delayed primary closure, split-thickness skin grafting, or flap coverage.

managementPearls.pearl: The single phrase that should trigger NF suspicion: 'This patient is too sick for the skin I see.' If a patient with a wound or soft tissue infection looks toxic (tachycardic, tachypneic, febrile, confused) but the skin looks 'not that bad,' assume NF until proven otherwise at surgical exploration.

The Finger Test (Fascial Separation Test): under local anesthesia in the ED or OR, make a 2 cm incision through the skin and subcutaneous tissue down to the deep fascia. Insert a sterile gloved finger and gently attempt to separate the fascia from the underlying muscle with blunt dissection. If the fascia separates easily (like peeling wallpaper), NF is present and radical debridement is indicated. If the fascia is firmly attached, NF is unlikely.

LRINEC <6 does NOT rule out NF. In the original validation study, sensitivity was 90%; in subsequent independent validations, sensitivity has been as low as 60-70%. The decision to explore is CLINICAL. If you think it could be NF, explore. It is far better to have a negative exploration (a 2 cm incision that heals in a few days) than to miss NF.

IV Clindamycin (600 mg TDS) is NOT optional in NF — it suppresses streptococcal and staphylococcal exotoxin production by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis at the 50S ribosomal subunit, independent of its antimicrobial effect. Penicillin alone is insufficient because the inoculum effect (high bacterial load) reduces its efficacy. The combination of penicillin + clindamycin is synergistic for streptococcal NF.

The first debridement is the most critical determinant of survival. Incomplete initial debridement is the most common surgical error. 'Debride until you are tired, and then debride some more.' Necrotic tissue left behind will continue to fuel the septic state. Surgeon experience matters: patients debrided by senior surgeons have better outcomes than those debrided by junior trainees.

In NF patients, intravascular volume depletion is massive — they can lose 5-10 L of fluid into the affected tissues within 24 hours. Aggressive fluid resuscitation is critical: 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, then dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation). Patients typically require 10-15 L of crystalloid in the first 24 hours. Vasopressors are a salvage measure for refractory shock after adequate fluid resuscitation.

NF is a reportable disease in many jurisdictions. Suspected cases should prompt public health notification, particularly if Group A Streptococcus is identified, as it can spread to close contacts and cause secondary cases of invasive streptococcal disease. Household contacts should receive chemoprophylaxis if symptomatic.

Analgesia in NF requires high-dose opioids (morphine 5-10 mg IV q4h or patient-controlled analgesia). The severity of pain is a diagnostic clue (pain out of proportion) and adequate analgesia facilitates patient cooperation. However, avoid masking the progression of pain (which may signal ongoing necrosis) with excessive sedation. Reassess pain pattern after each debridement.

In the resource-limited setting, when LRINEC and CT are unavailable, the 'bedside clinical score' can help: (1) pain out of proportion (2 points), (2) tachycardia >100 (1 point), (3) fever >38C (1 point), (4) crepitus (3 points), (5) hemorrhagic bullae (2 points). Score >=4: high suspicion for NF, explore. This simple tool can aid decision-making in rural district hospitals.

operativeNuances.nuance: The incision for NF exploration should be generous and placed over the area of maximal tenderness or the most abnormal skin. Once the diagnosis is confirmed at exploration, the incision must be extended radially in all directions until healthy fascia is encountered at every margin. The 'rule of thumb for margins': the incision should extend at least 5 cm beyond the visible skin changes, because the fascial necrosis extends 5-10 cm beyond the skin changes. After debridement, the wound is packed open with saline-moistened gauze (changed every 8-12 hours). NPWT can be applied after the first relook if the wound is clean. The dressing should be changed in the operating room for the first 2-3 procedures to allow re-assessment and re-debridement under ideal conditions.

In NF of the extremity, the fasciotomy incision must extend through all involved compartments. Standard fasciotomy incisions for the leg: anterolateral (for anterior and lateral compartments) and posteromedial (for superficial and deep posterior compartments). The skin incision should be longer than the fasciotomy to ensure complete decompression. Muscle viability is assessed by the 'Four Cs': Color (red > pink > dusky > black), Contractility (contracts on electrocautery stimulation > non-contractile), Consistency (firm > mushy), and Circulation (bright red bleeding from cut edges > no bleeding). Non-viable muscle must be debrided back to viable, bleeding muscle.

For NF of the upper extremity, use a standard volar forearm incision (from the antecubital fossa to the carpal tunnel) and a dorsal forearm incision. Decompress the carpal tunnel if median nerve symptoms are present. For hand involvement, use Bruner incisions (zigzag) over each involved digit and longitudinal incisions in the palm. The thenar and hypothenar spaces must be explored separately through their respective incisions.

In truncal NF (abdominal wall, chest wall, flank, or back), the debridement must extend through all layers of necrotic soft tissue down to healthy, bleeding muscle fascia or muscle. Remove the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and superficial fascia (Scarpa or Camper fascia) in continuity. The deep fascia (investing fascia of the muscle) must be inspected carefully — if it is edematous, gray, or separates easily from muscle, it must be removed as well. Protect the underlying muscle at all costs.

When the NF has extended to the perineum (Fournier variant), a diverting colostomy should be strongly considered, especially if the anal sphincter is involved. A loop sigmoid colostomy in the left lower quadrant is the simplest and most effective option. Fecal diversion dramatically reduces ongoing wound contamination, reduces the number of debridements needed, and accelerates wound healing.

prognosis.overallMortality: 20-40%; higher if >48 hours from onset to surgery (OR 3.0), age >60 years, diabetes, or septic shock at presentation
prognosis.amputationRate: 10-20% for extremity involvement; higher if Clostridium myonecrosis or delayed presentation
prognosis.numberOfDebridements: Mean 2-5 operations to achieve negative margins
prognosis.longTermMorbidity: Extensive scarring, limb dysfunction, need for reconstructive surgery, body image disturbance, psychological sequelae (PTSD)
prognosis.kenyanConsiderations: Higher mortality (estimated 30-50%) due to delayed presentation, limited ICU capacity, and high prevalence of diabetes and HIV
